1: shimĕʿû-zōʾt kol-hāʿammîm — hear this, all peoples! The universal address of a wisdom psalm — not just Israel but kol-hāʿammîm (all the peoples). haʾăzînû kol-yōshĕbê ḥāled — listen, all who live in the world. Ḥāled (the world, the temporal world — from a root meaning duration; the world as the sphere of transient time; cf. Psalms 17:14: mēḥeled ḥelqām, their portion in this life). The audience is as wide as humanity itself, because the psalm's subject (mortality and wealth's impotence before death) is universal.
2: gam-bĕnê ʾādām gam-bĕnê-ʾîsh — the humble and the great alike. Bĕnê ʾādām (sons of Adam — the common person, humanity in its generic form) + bĕnê ʾîsh (sons of a man of rank — the distinguished, the notable). The Hebrew idiom distinguishes commoner from aristocrat — both are addressed. yaḥad ʿāshîr wĕʾebyôn — the rich and the poor together. Yaḥad (together — they are unified in the audience, as they will be unified in death, v12). The wisdom that follows applies to all without distinction.
3: pî yĕdabbēr ḥokhĕmôt — my mouth will speak wisdom. The wisdom-genre self-identification: the speaker announces he will offer ḥokhĕmôt (wisdom — plural of ḥokhĕmāh: wisdoms, wisdom-speech in all its forms). wĕhagût libbî tĕbûnôt — and the reflection of my heart will be understanding. Hagût (meditation, reflection — from hāgāh: to murmur, to meditate) of the lēb (heart = the center of thought and understanding). Tĕbûnôt (understandings — plural: multiple facets of understanding). The heart's meditation as the source of what the mouth will speak — wisdom is not imported but grown internally.
4: ʾaṭṭeh lĕmāshāl ʾoznî — I bend my ear to a proverb. Māshāl (proverb, wisdom-saying — the generic term for wisdom-literature; from māshal: to rule, to be like). The act of bending the ear (nāṭāh ʾōzen) to a proverb — listening to receive wisdom. ʾeptaḥ bĕkhinnôr ḥîdātî — I will open my riddle with the harp. Ḥîdāh (riddle, dark saying, enigmatic wisdom — the indirect wisdom that requires insight to unpack; from ḥûd: to propose a riddle, cf. Samson's riddle, Judges 14:12-19). The pairing of māshāl and ḥîdāh signals the highest wisdom-register (cf. Psalms 78:2). The harp (kinnôr) as the instrument of prophetic inspiration (1 Samuel 10:5; 2 Kings 3:15).
5: lāmmāh ʾîrāʾ bîmê rāʿ — why should I be afraid in evil days? The existential question the psalm addresses: what is the ground of fearlessness when evil days come? ʿăwōn ʿăqēbay yĕsûbbênî — when the guilt of my accusers closes in around me. ʿĂqēb (heel — those who trip at the heel, who pursue closely from behind; from ʿāqab: to supplant, to overtake from behind). The accusers (or the treacherous) surrounding the Psalmist. The answer to why not to fear occupies the rest of the psalm: because wealth cannot ransom, and death makes all equal, trust in God transcends the threat.
6: haboṭĕḥîm ʿal-ḥêlām — those who trust in their wealth. Bāṭaḥ ʿal (to trust in, rely upon — placing one's weight on). Ḥayil (wealth, strength, resources — the accumulated capital in all its forms). ûbĕrōb ʿoshĕrām yithallālû — and boast of their great riches. Hālal (hitpael: to boast, to praise oneself — the same root as hallelujah inverted: self-praise instead of God-praise). The object of the self-boasting is ʿōsher (riches, wealth). The irony: those who will not escape death (vv7-12) are those who trust in wealth and boast of riches.
7: ʾāḥ lōʾ-phādōh yiphĕdeh ʾîsh — truly no one can by any means ransom another person. The emphatic negation: ʾāḥ lōʾ-phādōh yiphĕdeh (surely no/by no means shall one ransom) — the infinitive absolute (phādōh) + imperfect (yiphĕdeh) expresses the categorical impossibility. Phādāh (to ransom, to buy back from danger/obligation/death — the redemption verb of the Exodus, Psalms 130:8). The ransom that no human can pay for another. lōʾ-yittēn lĕʾĕlōhîm kophĕrô — or give God a payment for their life. Kōpher (the ransom price — the substitute payment that averts death, as in Exodus 30:12). No ransom sufficient to God can be offered by a human.
8: wĕyāqar pidyon nafshām — for the price of their life is too costly. Pidyon (ransom — from phādāh) + yāqār (too costly, too high — beyond affordability). wĕḥādal lĕʿôlām — and no payment will ever be enough. Ḥādal (to cease, to give up — the payment must be abandoned as an impossible task). The economic metaphor: the ransom price exceeds all possible human resources; it must be given up as unachievable forever.
9: wîḥî-ʿôd lāneṣaḥ — that one could live on forever. The goal that the ransom would achieve — continued life — is declared impossible. lōʾ yirʾeh hashshāḥat — and not see the pit. Shāḥat (the pit — the grave, Sheol as a pit; the destination of every unransomed life). No humanly-funded ransom can prevent the grave.
10: kî yirʾeh ḥăkhāmîm yāmûtû — he sees that even the wise die. The empirical observation: the wise (ḥăkhāmîm) die like everyone else — wisdom does not provide exemption from death. yaḥad kĕsîl wābāʿar yōbēdû — the fool and the senseless alike perish. Kĕsîl (fool — the morally obtuse) + bāʿar (brute, the senseless person of animal-like behavior, cf. Psalms 92:6). All three — wise, fool, brute — perish together. wĕʿāzĕbû lāʾăḥērîm ḥêlām — and leave their wealth behind for others. The wealth (ḥayil) that was trusted (v6) and boasted of cannot be carried into death — it passes to others (ʾăḥērîm: strangers).
11: qirbām bāttêmô lĕʿôlām — in their hearts they imagined their homes would last forever. Qereb (inner parts, heart — what they thought inside). Their homes (bāttêmô: their houses) imagined as permanent. mishkĕnōtām lĕdōr wādōr — their dwelling places to all generations. The fantasy of dynastic permanence — houses and estates passing through all generations. qārĕʾû bishĕmôtām ʿălê ʾădāmôt — they called their lands after their own names. The act of naming the land after oneself — the ancient equivalent of leaving one's name on a building or institution — as a gesture of claiming permanence that death will defeat.
12: wĕʾādām bîqār bĕlî-yālîn — man in all his honor will not last overnight. Bîqār (in honor, in dignity — the splendor of wealth and position). Lōʾ yālîn (will not spend the night — will not remain through even a single night of time; the brevity expressed by a single night's lodging). nidmeh kabbe-hēmôt nidĕmû — he is like the animals that perish. Dāmāh (to be like, to resemble) + bĕhēmôt (animals, cattle) — the human without divine connection is likened to beasts that simply die and are gone. This refrain appears again at v20, with one significant variation.
13: zeh darkām kĕsel lāmô — this is the fate of those who are confident in themselves. Kesel (foolish confidence, self-reliance — from the root of kĕsîl: the fool who trusts in the wrong thing). wĕʾaḥărêhem bĕphêhem yirṣû selāh — and after them people applaud their talk. Those who come after them (ʾaḥărêhem) approve of their path (bĕphêhem yirṣû: they find pleasure in their words). The fool's path gets inherited and continued by others who find it appealing. The selah marks a moment for the listener to absorb this cycle of folly.
14: kaṣṣōʾn lashshĕʾôl shattû — like sheep they are headed for Sheol. The driving of sheep: shātat (to drive, appoint) — they are herded toward Sheol as sheep are driven to their destination. māwet yirʿēm — death will be their shepherd. Rāʿāh (to shepherd, to pasture) — Death as shepherd is the psalm's darkest inversion: the God who is shepherd (Psalms 23:1; Psalms 80:1) is here replaced by Death as the shepherd of those who trust in wealth. wîrdû-bām yeshārîm laboqer — and the upright will have dominion over them in the morning. The eschatological reversal at the morning: the yeshārîm (the upright ones) rule over the formerly proud rich. wĕṣûrām lĕbattôt sheʾôl — their bodies will decay in Sheol, with no home left for them. The end of the household fantasy of v11: no home (zĕbul: dwelling, lordly residence) remains.
15: ʾakh-ʾĕlōhîm yiphĕdeh nafshî miyyad-shĕʾôl — but God will ransom my soul from the grip of Sheol. The pivot verse — ʾakh (but, surely — the contrast particle). Everything vv7-9 said was impossible (lōʾ-phādōh yiphĕdeh: none can ransom another), God will do: yiphĕdeh (he will ransom). The vocabulary is exact: the impossible human ransom becomes the possible divine ransom. kî yiqqāḥēnî selāh — for he will take me to himself. Lāqaḥ (to take) — the word used of Enoch's divine taking (wayyiqqah ʾōtô ʾĕlōhîm, Genesis 5:24) and of Elijah's translation (2 Kings 2:1-11). The verb carries the weight of those two precedents: being taken by God is the vocabulary of post-mortem rescue. The selah marks this as the most weighty statement in the psalm.
16: ʾal-tîrāʾ kî-yaʿăshîr ʾîsh — do not be frightened when someone becomes rich. The practical application of vv7-15: the command not to fear (yārēʾ) the enrichment of another. kî-yirbbeh kĕbôd bêtô — when the splendor of their household grows. Kābôd (glory, splendor — here applied to a household's impressive accumulation). The apparently threatening success of the wealthy person is the context of fear — the psalm counsels the right response.
17: kî lōʾ bĕmôtô yiqqaḥ hakkōl — for when they die they take nothing with them. The reason not to fear: at death, all the accumulated wealth (hakkōl: everything) is left behind. lōʾ-yērēd ʾaḥărāyw kĕbôdô — their glory will not follow them down. The reversal: the glory (kābôd) that was growing (v16) will not descend with the person into Sheol — it stays in the world above and passes to others (v10: lāʾăḥērîm).
18: kî-nafshô bĕḥayyāyw yĕbārek — though in their lifetime they counted themselves fortunate. Bārak nafshô (to bless oneself — to congratulate oneself, to pronounce oneself well-off; the self-congratulation of the prosperous). wĕyôdûkhā kî-têṭîb lākh — and people praised them for prospering. Yādāh (hiph: to praise) when things go well (kî-têṭîb lākh: because it is good for you). The social praise of prosperity: people praise the successful as long as success lasts.
19: tābôʾ ʿad-dôr ʾăbôtāyw — they will go to join their ancestors. The inevitable reunion with the dead — the same Sheol that received their forefathers receives them. ʿad-neṣaḥ lōʾ yirʾû-ʾôr — and never again see the light. ʾôr (light — the light of the living world, of divine favor; cf. Psalms 36:9: bĕʾôrĕkhā nirʾeh-ʾôr). The loss of light is the definitive characteristic of Sheol (cf. Psalms 88:12; Job 10:21-22). The self-praising, wealth-trusting person goes into permanent darkness.
20: wĕʾādām bîqār wĕlōʾ yābîn nidmeh kabbe-hēmôt nidĕmû — man in all his honor, but without understanding, is like the animals that perish. The refrain returns from v12 with one variation: v12 has bĕlî-yālîn (will not remain/lodge) but v20 has wĕlōʾ yābîn (without understanding). The final verse specifies what makes human honor equivalent to animal death: the absence of bîn (understanding/discernment — the wisdom-faculty). Honor without understanding ends in animal-death. V15's divine ransom is the alternative for those who do understand.
